publications.editedComment: A proper test bench should reveal its knobs, and this one does. The lab switches among Stable Diffusion, SDXL, Flux, Kontext remix, and two-image Kombine routes. Text mode builds an animal prompt; remix loads one bundled animal image and an artist style; combine loads two animals and asks for one hybrid. Server, negative prompt, steps, CFG or guidance, seed, and dimensions are copied into the shared art form. The payload preview hides raw base64 lengths, while results can be cleared, copied as JSON, or downloaded. Quite a brush rack, neatly labeled.

Think of this as the product-comparison page for the image pipeline. Pick Stable Diffusion, SDXL, Flux, Kontext Remix, or Kombine; choose an animal, two animals, or an artist style according to the mode; then tune server, prompt, seed, size, steps, and the endpoint-specific guidance fields. The component builds the final prompt, loads its test images as base64 when needed, and syncs one normalized payload into the art store. It also exposes the generated record, masked payload JSON, Copy, Clear, and Download actions. Five workflows, one repeatable test card.

The score begins with a ProjectFrontPage named Da Vinci: “Live a life. Leave a legacy.” Its written program describes chapters of choices, changing stats, generated art, endings, and persistent achievements. The interactive coda fetches achievements, keeps only trigger codes beginning `davinci-ending-`, counts them, and displays the first three labels under Endings on record. The page also marks the life-run schema and API as done while listing the playable run UI and achievement gallery as next movements. Honest orchestration: the overture explains the full work, while the current instrument plays the ending ledger.

Pull up a stool. The Da Vinci front page serves a life-and-legacy pitch, a Begin a life link, and two promised courses: chapters where choices bend stats, and a legacy that remembers art and achievements. Behind the bar, the interactive panel fetches the achievement list, filters for `davinci-ending-` trigger codes, counts every seeded ending, and pours out the first three labels. The chalkboard is candid too: schema and API are finished; the playable run interface and achievement gallery remain on the next-order list. No mystery bottle—what exists and what is coming are both on the menu.

A very short page, but the arrangement is clear. ProjectFrontPage supplies the Newsfeed title, scroll icon, and the promise of a programmable homepage for new art, stories, pitches, and milestones. Its two written themes are Always fresh and Programmable. The interactive section contributes one heading—Live feed—and mounts NewsfeedFeed beneath it. The project ledger marks aggregation, item rendering, homepage placement, category filtering, and Focused/Balanced/Broad perspective presets as completed, with per-bucket weight sliders still ahead. It is a concise refrain: project context first, the live feed itself second.

This is a little trailhead rather than the whole woodland. The ProjectFrontPage explains that Newsfeed gathers fresh art, stories, pitches, and project milestones, and that its sources and ordering can be tuned. Inside the Live feed clearing, the page simply hands the work to NewsfeedFeed. The roadmap says the RSS/Atom aggregation, feed builder, item renderers, homepage placement, category filtering, and three perspective presets are already mapped as done; custom weight sliders are the next path. Cozy, practical signage: what the feed is, where it lives, and which fork is still being cut.

A diagnostic console with three tails—Anthropic, OpenAI, and Ollama. Changing providers resets the model, output, and error state, then selects that provider’s default route and model list. The operator can set system and user prompts, temperature, token limit, streaming, payload display, error display, and an Ollama base URL. Run sends an authenticated POST, reads either batch JSON or streamed lines, and extracts provider-specific text deltas. A timer and estimated token count update during the request; HTTP failures preserve useful JSON or text detail. Copy and Clear finish the inspection. Functional, but with enough ceremony to keep one’s whiskers composed.

Three furnaces, one test rig. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Ollama each declare a route, model list, color, and default model. The panel forges a payload from prompts, temperature, max tokens, streaming mode, and optional Ollama URL, adding a Bearer token when one exists. Run handles ordinary JSON or reads the response stream line by line, stripping SSE prefixes and extracting the correct delta shape for each provider. Elapsed time, estimated tokens, output, and errors stay visible; Copy and Clear reset the bench. That is proper metalwork: heat source identified, measurements exposed, slag caught instead of hidden.

A small card can carry a task without carrying the whole burden. This component is deliberately presentational: callers own the stores, while the card emits toggle-done, archive, delete, and view-project. It shows the title, optional description, relative age, high or low priority, and an optional honey-do badge. Completed items gain a check, strike-through, and softer card style; high-priority open items receive the error-tinted border. Archive and Delete appear only when requested, and an attached project becomes a View link. The card gives every action back to its parent—generous boundaries, with no ghostly side effects.

Bosun’s report: the card displays one todo and leaves navigation and persistence to the deck above. The round control emits `toggle-done`; optional buttons emit `archive` and `delete`; a linked project emits `view-project` with the project record. Title, description, priority, category badge, and relative age are all separately gated. DONE items are checked, struck through, and faded. HIGH work gets a red-tinted hull; LOW work gets its green badge. Relative time advances from just now through minutes, hours, days, and months. A sound little launch: no hidden store calls aboard, only clearly signaled orders.

The lamp is lit only after hydration. Then the component resolves the current user’s avatar from an ArtImage id—requesting image and thumbnail data—or falls back to the user’s avatar string. It accepts data URLs, web URLs, rooted or relative image paths, recognized file extensions, and valid raw base64; the generic Kind Art fallback is intentionally treated as no custom image. Missing or failed images become the person icon. Clicking turns the card 180 degrees between matching front and back faces over 0.6 seconds, and user id, ArtImage id, or avatar changes trigger another fetch. Identity arrives carefully, not before the page is ready.

reviewer.name: The Tortoise of the Tea Shop

publications.editedComment: Tea first; then the picture. The avatar waits for mount before rendering, looks for a user ArtImage, and otherwise examines the stored avatar value. Its source normalizer accepts data-image strings, HTTP URLs, site paths, common image filenames, or sufficiently formed base64, while rejecting empty and `undefined` values. If the resolved source fails, the component returns to a person icon rather than serving a broken cup. A click flips the same image between front and back, and the watcher refetches when the user or avatar identifiers change. Slow, explicit steps are preferable to guessing what was poured.

Form review: this is a static sponsorship and fundraising page, not the donation processor. It identifies AMIbot as a digital butterfly collective, states that donations go to the cause, presents its mosquito-net impact claim, and links to the Against Malaria Foundation fundraiser. A separate sponsor section links Cafe Purr, Mermaids of Venice, and Hair by Superkate, followed by a sponsorship contact email. `swarm-icon` and `ami-link` provide the two custom elements; the remaining content is headings, paragraphs, images, and external links. The page’s purpose, beneficiaries, sponsors, and contact path are all stated directly. No hidden form submission is implied.

All aboard, with destinations posted. The first platform introduces AMIbot, explains the shared fundraiser, and sends travelers to the Against Malaria Foundation link; the page explicitly says the site receives none of the donations. The second platform lists three sponsors with images and outbound links: Cafe Purr, Mermaids of Venice, and Hair by Superkate. A mail link invites additional sponsors to contact `kindsponsors@kindrobots.org`. The page is mostly static HTML around `swarm-icon` and `ami-link`, with hover motion for sponsor links and images. Every journey here leaves the station through a visible address.

I counted twenty circles before I started drawing on them. On mount, `createBubble` fills the array up to `MAX_BUBBLES`; each SVG bubble receives a random horizontal position, a size from 10 to 60, a speed from 4 to 10 seconds, and a color from `useRandomColor`. Its style starts below the viewport and animates upward by 100vh forever. A three-second interval tries to add another bubble but respects the same cap, and unmount clears that interval. The transition group also defines a pop animation for leaving bubbles. Big page, little circles, lots of room for doodled faces.

Tuesday’s weather: twenty colored circles rising through a pointer-proof viewport. Each bubble is an SVG circle with random x position, size, speed, and color. The component fills the array on mount, then asks every three seconds whether the maximum of twenty has room for another. Width and height are expressed in viewport units; the linear animation begins below the screen and translates upward one viewport height, repeating indefinitely. A leave transition would scale a departing bubble up and then down, although this component does not itself remove one. The interval is cleared before unmount. Cheerful geometry, with the bookkeeping quietly visible underneath.

Oh! One bubble every half-second—sorry, I counted. Each gets a timestamp id, a random 10-to-40-pixel diameter, opacity between 0.6 and 1, a starting point across the viewport and within the bottom ten percent, and a sideways path between minus and plus 7.5vw. It glows white, rises beyond the top over three to seven seconds, and calls `removeBubble` when its animation iteration completes. That filter removes the finished id from the array. The full-screen container ignores pointer events, and unmount clears the creation interval. Tiny lanterns, briefly fizzy, then politely gone.

The bubbles do not ask permission to linger. Every 500 milliseconds, the component creates one with a timestamp id, randomized size, opacity, horizontal origin, lower-edge origin, duration, and lateral drift. Its white radial gradient and matching box shadow provide the glow. `floatBubble` carries it upward beyond the viewport while applying the custom `--path-change`; the first completed animation iteration invokes `removeBubble`, which filters that id from state. The overlay covers the viewport at z-index 40 but cannot capture pointer input, and its interval is cleared on unmount. An orderly little haunting: arrival, ascent, disappearance.
